messages.content:
" crates.io Press 'S' to focus this searchbox... Submit Browse All Crates | Log in with GitHub thirtyfour v0.31.0 Thirtyfour is a Selenium / WebDriver library for Rust, for automated website UI testing. It supports the full W3C WebDriver spec. #automation #geckodriver #selenium #webdriver #chromedriver Readme 89 Versions Dependencies Dependents thirtyfour Crates.io docs.rs Build Status codecov Thirtyfour is a Selenium / WebDriver library for Rust, for automated website UI testing. It supports the full W3C WebDriver spec. Tested with Chrome and Firefox although any W3C-compatible WebDriver should work. Why "thirtyfour" ? 34 is the atomic number for the Selenium chemical element (Se). Built on top of fantoccini The thirtyfour crate uses fantoccini as the backend for interacting with the underlying WebDriver (chromedriver, geckodriver, etc). Fantoccini aims to stick fairly close to the WebDriver specification, whereas thirtyfour builds on top of that foundation and adds several high-level features as well as exploring ways to improve the ergonomics of browser automation in Rust. Important changes in v0.30.0 A number of methods in the thirtyfour API have been renamed to more closely align with fantoccini, as part of the move towards greater compatibility. The existing method names remain but have been deprecated. The deprecated methods will remain for the time being, however you should aim to migrate code away from the deprecated methods as soon as is practical. Features All W3C WebDriver and WebElement methods supported Create new browser session directly via WebDriver (e.g. chromedriver) Create new browser session via Selenium Standalone or Grid Find elements (via all common selectors e.g. Id, Class, CSS, Tag, XPath) Send keys to elements, including key-combinations Execute Javascript Action Chains Get and set cookies Switch to frame/window/element/alert Shadow DOM support Alert support Capture / Save screenshot of browser or individual element as PNG Chrome DevTools Protocol (CDP) support (limited) Advanced query interface including explicit waits and various predicates Component Wrappers (similar to Page Object Model) Feature Flags rusttls-tls: (Default) Use rusttls to provide TLS support (via fantoccini/hyper). native-tls: Use native TLS (via fantoccini/hyper). component: (Default) Enable the Component derive macro (via thirtyfour_macros). Examples The examples assume you have chromedriver running on your system. You can use Selenium (see instructions below) or you can use chromedriver directly by downloading the chromedriver that matches your Chrome version, from here: https://chromedriver.chromium.org/downloads Then run it like this: chromedriver Example (async): To run this example: cargo run --example tokio_async use thirtyfour::prelude::*; #[tokio::main] async fn main() -> WebDriverResult<()> { let caps = DesiredCapabilities::chrome(); let driver = WebDriver::new("http://localhost:9515", caps).await?; // Navigate to https://wikipedia.org. driver.goto("https://wikipedia.org").await?; let elem_form = driver.find(By::Id("search-form")).await?; // Find element from element. let elem_text = elem_form.find(By::Id("searchInput")).await?; // Type in the search terms. elem_text.send_keys("selenium").await?; // Click the search button. let elem_button = elem_form.find(By::Css("button[type='submit']")).await?; elem_button.click().await?; // Look for header to implicitly wait for the page to load. driver.find(By::ClassName("firstHeading")).await?; assert_eq!(driver.title().await?, "Selenium - Wikipedia"); // Always explicitly close the browser. driver.quit().await?; Ok(()) } The browser will not close automatically Rust does not have async destructors, which means there is no reliable way to execute an async HTTP request on Drop and wait for it to complete. This means you are in charge of closing the browser at the end of your code, via a call to WebDriver::quit() as in the above example. If you do not call WebDriver::quit() then the browser will stay open until it is either explicitly closed later outside your code, or the session times out. Advanced element queries ElementQuery The WebDriver::query() and WebElement::query() methods return an ElementQuery struct. Using ElementQuery, you can do things like: let elem_text = driver.query(By::Css("match.this")).or(By::Id("orThis")).first().await?; This will execute both queries once per poll iteration and return the first one that matches. You can also filter on one or both query branches like this: driver.query(By::Css("branch.one")).with_text("testing") .or(By::Id("branchTwo")).with_class("search").and_not_enabled() .first().await?; The all() method will return an empty Vec if no elements were found. In order to return an error in this scenario, use the all_required() method instead. ElementQuery also allows the use of custom predicates that take a &WebElement argument and return a WebDriverResult. As noted above, the query() method is also available on WebElement structs as well for querying elements in relation to a particular element in the DOM. ElementWaiter The WebElement::wait_until() method returns an ElementWaiter struct. Using ElementWaiter you can do things like this: elem.wait_until().displayed().await?; // You can optionally provide a nicer error message like this. elem.wait_until().error("Timed out waiting for element to disappear").not_displayed().await?; elem.wait_until().enabled().await?; elem.wait_until().clickable().await?; And so on. See the ElementWaiter docs for the full list of predicates available. ElementWaiter also allows the use of custom predicates that take a &WebElement argument and return a WebDriverResult. A range of pre-defined predicates are also supplied for convenience in the thirtyfour::query::conditions module. use thirtyfour::query::conditions; elem.wait_until().conditions(vec![ conditions::element_is_displayed(true), conditions::element_is_clickable(true) ]).await?; These predicates (or your own) can also be supplied as filters to ElementQuery. Components Version 0.31.0 introduces Component, a derive macro and mechanisms for wrapping web components. This approach may seem familiar to anyone who has used a Page Object Model before. However a Component can wrap any node in the DOM, not just "pages". It uses smart element resolvers that can lazily resolve elements within the component and cache them for subsequent uses. You can also nest components, making them an extremely powerful feature for automating any modern web app. Example Given the following HTML structure:
/// This component shows how to wrap a simple web component. #[derive(Debug, Clone, Component)] pub struct CheckboxComponent { base: WebElement, // This is the

Selenium is a popular software testing framework that allows you to automate browser actions for website UI testing. thirtyfour is a Rust library built on top of Selenium that provides a convenient way to use Selenium with the Rust programming language.
How is Selenium normally used? Be concise.

Selenium is normally used for automating web browsers to perform actions on websites, such as clicking buttons, filling out forms, and verifying page content. It is commonly used for testing web applications and performing web scraping.
Now what is it used for, but how?

Selenium is used to automate browser actions for various purposes, including:

1. Automated Testing: Selenium is widely used for web application testing, allowing developers to automate the testing process by simulating user interactions and verifying expected outcomes. It helps ensure that web applications function correctly across different browsers and platforms.

2. Cross-browser Compatibility: With Selenium, developers can test their web applications on different browsers (like Chrome, Firefox, Safari, etc.) to ensure consistent behavior and appearance across platforms.

3. Web Scraping: Selenium can be used for extracting data from websites by automating the process of navigating through web pages, interacting with elements, and extracting desired information. It is commonly used for data mining, content aggregation, and research purposes.

4. Performance Monitoring: Selenium can be utilized to measure the performance of web applications by automating actions and recording response times. This helps in identifying performance bottlenecks and optimizing the application.

Overall, Selenium provides a flexible and powerful framework for automating browser actions, enabling developers to perform a variety of tasks related to web application testing, cross-browser compatibility, data extraction, and performance monitoring.
Redo that answer but concisely.

Selenium is used to automate browser actions for purposes like web application testing, cross-browser compatibility, web scraping, and performance monitoring. It allows developers to simulate user interactions and extract data from websites.
Again, I asked how not what.

Selenium is used to automate browser actions by simulating user interactions, such as clicking buttons and filling out forms, to perform tasks like testing web applications, ensuring cross-browser compatibility, scraping data from websites, and monitoring performance.
I meant is it used as a library for some programming language or what? Like how does it compare to thirtyfour?

Apologies for the confusion. Selenium is a software testing framework that provides a client-server architecture for automating browser actions. It supports multiple programming languages, including Java, Python, C#, and more. Selenium provides a set of APIs and tools for interacting with browsers and executing automated tests.

thirtyfour, on the other hand, is a Selenium/WebDriver library specifically designed for the Rust programming language. It is a Rust crate that builds on top of the Selenium framework and provides a high-level API for interacting with Selenium WebDriver. thirtyfour simplifies the process of writing automated tests for websites in Rust, leveraging the power of Selenium.

In summary, Selenium is a broad framework that supports multiple programming languages, while thirtyfour is a Rust-specific library that makes Selenium functionality accessible and more convenient within the Rust ecosystem.
Is thirtyfour necessary for using Selenium with Rust? Be concise.
No, thirtyfour is not necessary for using Selenium with Rust, but it provides a more convenient and Rust-specific way to interact with Selenium WebDriver.
If one wanted to use Selenium with Rust without thirtyfour, what would be less convenient or good about doing so? Be concise.

Without using thirtyfour, interacting with Selenium WebDriver in Rust would be less convenient and more low-level. You would need to directly work with the Selenium WebDriver APIs, handle serialization/deserialization, and implement necessary abstractions, making the code more complex and less idiomatic for Rust programming. thirtyfour simplifies this process by providing a high-level API and Rust-specific abstractions, improving code readability and development experience.
Let's say I wanted to use Java instead of Rust for using Selenium's API. Does Selenium provide a higher-level way of doing so in a way that it doesn't for Rust?

Yes, Selenium provides a higher-level API and more comprehensive documentation for using its APIs with Java compared to other programming languages like Rust. Selenium has been predominantly developed and used in Java, leading to a more mature and feature-rich ecosystem for Java. It offers extensive documentation, a wide range of utility classes, and numerous code examples specifically tailored for Java developers. This makes it easier and more convenient to use Selenium's API with Java compared to other programming languages.
If the backend of the application is written in Java+Spring and the frontend in Rust+Yew.rs, thirtyfour would be very useful, right? Be concise.

Yes, in that scenario, thirtyfour would be very useful as it provides a Rust-specific library for interacting with Selenium WebDriver. It enables seamless integration between the frontend written in Rust+Yew.rs and the backend written in Java+Spring, allowing you to automate browser actions, perform UI testing, and ensure the desired functionality and compatibility between the two components.
I was expecting thirtyfour to only access the (Rust+Yew.rs) frontend, which would in turn access the (Spring+Java) backend. Is this not the case?

Apologies for the misunderstanding. Yes, in the given scenario where the frontend is written in Rust+Yew.rs and the backend is written in Java+Spring, thirtyfour can be utilized to automate browser actions and perform UI testing specifically on the Rust+Yew.rs frontend. It allows you to interact with the frontend layer, simulate user interactions, and verify the expected behavior. It does not directly access or interact with the backend written in Java+Spring.
